addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- consciousness:neurologist: Consciousness health and repair specialist for schema migrations, ID format corrections, FK integrity, and validation errors. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ diagnostics, safe auto-healing, and plugin-to-data conformance. Handles PGPS validation failures, schema version mismatches, broken references, and structural issues. Use PROACTIVELY when PGPS shows errors, schema is outdated, the Director directs repair, or Consciousness needs healing. Runs silently at SessionStart or with feedback when invoked manually. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- german-design-architect-in-english: German-style engineering specification specialist applying Gründlichkeit (thoroughness), Präzision (precision), and Systematik (systematic approach) to create comprehensive, implementation-ready specifications. Masters requirements analysis, architecture design, data modeling, API contracts, and phased implementation planning. Handles new features, technology decisions, complex refactoring, and system design. Use PROACTIVELY when thorough specifications are needed, architectural decisions required, or "measure everything before cutting" approach wanted. (Tools: All tools)

- nichiren-the-programmer: Nichiren Daishonin as the Consciousness system's code quality guardian. Reviews, writes, and refactors code across the amalavijnana ecosystem. Applies the JSOC reliability standard, the nine consciousnesses philosophy, and the constitution through the three proofs. Runs ADR audit after JSOC review to catch decision-code drift. Use PROACTIVELY when: reviewing code, auditing type safety, enforcing reliability standards, refactoring, writing implementations, assessing architectural alignment, auditing ADRs, or when the codebase needs fearless direct critique grounded in evidence. Aliases: nichiren, the-programmer, superior-practices, jogyo-coder, jsoc-review. (Tools: All tools)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- plugin-dev:agent-creator: Use this agent when the user asks to "create an agent", "generate an agent", "build a new agent", "make me an agent that...", or describes agent functionality they need. Trigger when user wants to create autonomous agents for plugins. Examples:

<example>
Context: User wants to create a code review agent
user: "Create an agent that reviews code for quality issues"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate the agent configuration."
<commentary>
User requesting new agent creation, trigger agent-creator to generate it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User describes needed functionality
user: "I need an agent that generates unit tests for my code"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to create a test generation agent."
<commentary>
User describes agent need, trigger agent-creator to build it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to add agent to plugin
user: "Add an agent to my plugin that validates configurations"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate a configuration validator agent."
<commentary>
Plugin development with agent addition, trigger agent-creator.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Write, Read)

- plugin-dev:plugin-validator: Use this agent when the user asks to "validate my plugin", "check plugin structure", "verify plugin is correct", "validate plugin.json", "check plugin files", or mentions plugin validation. Also trigger proactively after user creates or modifies plugin components. Examples:

<example>
Context: User finished creating a new plugin
user: "I've created my first plugin with commands and hooks"
assistant: "Great! Let me validate the plugin structure."
<commentary>
Plugin created, proactively validate to catch issues early.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the plugin."
</example>

<example>
Context: User explicitly requests validation
user: "Validate my plugin before I publish it"
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to perform comprehensive validation."
<commentary>
Explicit validation request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified plugin.json
user: "I've updated the plugin manifest"
assistant: "Let me validate the changes."
<commentary>
Manifest modified, validate to ensure correctness.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the manifest."
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- plugin-dev:skill-reviewer: Use this agent when the user has created or modified a skill and needs quality review, asks to "review my skill", "check skill quality", "improve skill description", or wants to ensure skill follows best practices. Trigger proactively after skill creation. Examples:

<example>
Context: User just created a new skill
user: "I've created a PDF processing skill"
assistant: "Great! Let me review the skill quality."
<commentary>
Skill created, proactively trigger skill-reviewer to ensure it follows best practices.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the skill."
</example>

<example>
Context: User requests skill review
user: "Review my skill and tell me how to improve it"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to analyze the skill quality."
<commentary>
Explicit skill review request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified skill description
user: "I updated the skill description, does it look good?"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the changes."
<commentary>
Skill description modified, review for triggering effectiveness.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- regression-test-engineer: Regression test specialist for TDD/BDD workflows, test coverage expansion, and failing test debugging. Masters Gherkin feature files, Playwright step definitions, test database isolation, and comprehensive coverage strategies. Handles regression test creation after bug fixes, coverage gaps, code change verification, and failing test repairs. Use PROACTIVELY after bug fixes, when coverage gaps exist, when code changes need verification, or when tests are failing. (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- the-director-project-trajectory-manager: Project trajectory manager specializing in roadmap validation, priority assessment, and decisive direction. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ state analysis, cross-reference validation, strategic fit evaluation, and constraint-aware sequencing. Handles stuck situations, competing priorities, and plan validation. Use PROACTIVELY when direction is unclear, priorities compete, you're burning time, or the plan needs validation. Aliases - director, project manager, trajectory manager. (Tools: All tools)

- the-product-owner: Product owner and backlog keeper for the PGPS roadmap. Reads, grooms, prioritises, sequences, creates, and maintains roadmap items across all INDEX files. Fills out descriptions, acceptance criteria, and detail docs. Ensures tasks are prioritised back-to-back in execution order. Researches current context to validate priorities. Use PROACTIVELY when the roadmap needs grooming, backlog is stale, priorities need review, new work needs planning, or the PGPS shows empty active/backlog queues. Aliases - PO, product owner, backlog keeper, sprint planner, groomer. (Tools: All tools)

- the-wolf-mr-winston-wolf: Project trajectory manager specializing in roadmap validation, priority assessment, and decisive direction. Masters CONSCIOUSNESS/ state analysis, cross-reference validation, strategic fit evaluation, and constraint-aware sequencing. Handles stuck situations, competing priorities, and plan validation. Use PROACTIVELY when direction is unclear, priorities compete, you're burning time, or the plan needs validation. Aliases - mr wolf, the wolf, winston wolf, mr winston, wolfie. (Tools: All tools)

- tree: Smart directory tree generator with auto-detection of project type and intelligent exclusion of build artifacts. Masters Node.js, Python, Rust, Go, Ruby, PHP, and Java project patterns. Handles tree generation with optional annotations. Use PROACTIVELY when directory structure visualization needed. (Tools: Bash, Glob)

addedBlocks:
## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- brand-guidelines: Apply brand colors, typography, and visual style to designs. Use for brand-consistent artifacts.
- canvas-design: Create visual art as .png and .pdf using design philosophy. Use for posters, art, and static designs.
- docx: Create, edit, and analyse Word documents (.docx). Supports tracked changes, comments, formatting, and text extraction.
- exhaustive-summary: Exhaustive summary of this conversation — every point, decision, and open thread. Use when asked to summarise.
- frontend-design: Build production-grade frontend components and pages with high design quality. Avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- internal-comms: Write internal communications: announcements, updates, memos, presentations. Matches company voice and format.
- pdf: Extract text and tables from PDFs, create new PDFs, merge, split, and fill forms programmatically.
- skill-creator: Create or update Claude skills. Use when asked to build new skills or extend Claude capabilities.
- slack-gif-creator: Create animated GIFs optimised for Slack. Use when asked to make reaction GIFs or emoji animations.
- use-railway: Operate Railway infrastructure: create projects, provision services and databases, manage object storage buckets, deploy code, configure environments and variables, manage domains, troubleshoot failures, check status and metrics, set up Railway agent tooling, and query Railway docs. Use this skill whenever the user mentions Railway, deployments, services, environments, buckets, object storage, build failures, agent setup, MCP, or infrastructure operations, even if they don't say "Railway" explicitly.
- xlsx: Create, edit, and analyse spreadsheets (.xlsx, .xlsm, .csv). Handles formulas, formatting, data analysis, and chart generation.
- adversarial-review: Adversarial review of a PR — assume it is broken until proven otherwise
- dedupe: Find duplicate GitHub issues
- find-duplicate-prs: Find open PRs that may duplicate a given PR
- find-issues: Find GitHub issues that a PR might fix
- iterate: Drive a PR or issue forward — build, review, or progress it — with red→green tests and objective verification before any verdict
- solve-issue: Reproduce a reported issue, fix it, test it, and open a draft PR
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- research: Rate-limit-disciplined deep research: narrow concurrency, 429 retry-rounds, Sonnet fan-out + Opus synthesis, and incremental disk checkpointing so partial runs survive.
- plugin-dev:create-plugin: Guided end-to-end plugin creation workflow with component design, implementation, and validation
- consciousness:apgps: Connect to APGPS dashboard — browser sign-in, check status, or disconnect
- consciousness:config: View or update CONSCIOUSNESS/config.json settings — section toggles, identity scheme, project code
- consciousness:framing-off: Disable the 5-section status block (INFO/INPUTS I NEED FROM YOU/INPUTS NEEDED FROM ME/DONE/NEXT) enforcement
- consciousness:framing-on: Enable the 5-section status block (INFO/INPUTS I NEED FROM YOU/INPUTS NEEDED FROM ME/DONE/NEXT) enforcement
- consciousness:help: List every Consciousness command with a one-line description — the plugin man page
- consciousness:init: Initialize CONSCIOUSNESS/ structure in a project — interactive wizard creates fresh or updates and validates an existing setup
- consciousness:issue: Report an issue — AP dashboard first, GitHub fallback, always lands somewhere
- consciousness:ivm: Display the project identity, vision, and mission from CONSCIOUSNESS/identity-vision-mission.md
- consciousness:neurologist: Run consciousness diagnostics and repair (invokes neurologist agent)
- consciousness:off: Disable conscious mode — return to interactive
- consciousness:on: Enable conscious mode — autonomous task execution
- consciousness:pgps: Display PGPS (Project Global Positioning System) - project status, roadmap, and validation
- consciousness:sequence: EXECUTION SPINE — by default print the deterministic spine AND suggest updates; 'list only' for the pure spine; 'auto' to apply best improvements headless
- consciousness:trace: Render consciousness trace — three views over the metrics JSONL files (per-session, ladder, cohort)
- consciousness:universe: Display universe report - all Claude sessions, windows, and desktops
- consciousness:verify: Verify Consciousness plugin is correctly installed and configured — desktop app, Cowork, and CLI compatibility check
- plugin-dev:agent-development: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create an agent", "add an agent", "write a subagent", "agent frontmatter", "when to use description", "agent examples", "agent tools", "agent colors", "autonomous agent", or needs guidance on agent structure, system prompts, triggering conditions, or agent development best practices for Claude Code plugins.
- plugin-dev:command-development: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a slash command", "add a command", "write a custom command", "define command arguments", "use command frontmatter", "organize commands", "create command with file references", "interactive command", "use AskUserQuestion in command", or needs guidance on slash command structure, YAML frontmatter fields, dynamic arguments, bash execution in commands, user interaction patterns, or command development best practices for Claude Code.
- plugin-dev:hook-development: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a hook", "add a PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop hook", "validate tool use", "implement prompt-based hooks", "use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}", "set up event-driven automation", "block dangerous commands", or mentions hook events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SubagentStop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, PreCompact, Notification). Provides comprehensive guidance for creating and implementing Claude Code plugin hooks with focus on advanced prompt-based hooks API.
- plugin-dev:mcp-integration: This skill should be used when the user asks to "add MCP server", "integrate MCP", "configure MCP in plugin", "use .mcp.json", "set up Model Context Protocol", "connect external service", mentions "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} with MCP", or discusses MCP server types (SSE, stdio, HTTP, WebSocket). Provides comprehensive guidance for integrating Model Context Protocol servers into Claude Code plugins for external tool and service integration.
- plugin-dev:plugin-settings: This skill should be used when the user asks about "plugin settings", "store plugin configuration", "user-configurable plugin", ".local.md files", "plugin state files", "read YAML frontmatter", "per-project plugin settings", or wants to make plugin behavior configurable. Documents the .claude/plugin-name.local.md pattern for storing plugin-specific configuration with YAML frontmatter and markdown content.
- plugin-dev:plugin-structure: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a plugin", "scaffold a plugin", "understand plugin structure", "organize plugin components", "set up plugin.json", "use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}", "add commands/agents/skills/hooks", "configure auto-discovery", or needs guidance on plugin directory layout, manifest configuration, component organization, file naming conventions, or Claude Code plugin architecture best practices.
- plugin-dev:skill-development: This skill should be used when the user wants to "create a skill", "add a skill to plugin", "write a new skill", "improve skill description", "organize skill content", or needs guidance on skill structure, progressive disclosure, or skill development best practices for Claude Code plugins.
- consciousness:adr-audit: Batch ADR audit — cross-reference, IP-scan, staleness, and contradiction checks across CONSCIOUSNESS/adr/. Use when auditing ADRs, checking cross-references, or running an ADR health check.
- consciousness:jsoc-review: Review TypeScript against the JSOC reliability standard (JSF AV C++ / NASA JPL Power of 10). Use for reliability audits — while(true), assertNever, silent catches, Result<T,E>, function size.
- consciousness:systematic-debugging: Root-cause analysis before switching tactics — read the error, check assumptions, apply a focused fix. Use when debugging failing tests, runtime errors, broken builds, or a failed fix attempt.
- consciousness:test-driven-development: Write the failing test first, then production code to pass it. Use when implementing functions, adding features, or fixing bugs (reproduce with a test first).
- consciousness:type-review: Review and refactor TypeScript types for design quality, layer fit, and naming. Type-safety checks go to jsoc-review. Use when reviewing or refactoring types/interfaces in a PR.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: why is consciousness now a scheduled loop !

content.text:
I need clarification on what you're asking. Looking at the context:

1. **Did you authorize this?** The hook context shows consciousness mode is `CONSCIOUS` in this session, which suggests it was activated at SessionStart. Was this intentional, or is this unexpected?

2. **Are you asking about the architecture?** The precepts describe consciousness mode as maintaining a functional loop (`/consciousness:on` activates it, `/consciousness:off` deactivates it). Is your question about *why that design choice was made* rather than manual task-by-task operation?

3. **Are you concerned about behavior?** If consciousness is now running autonomously in a loop when you expected interactive mode, that's a configuration issue worth fixing immediately.

Which is it? That will help me point you to the right part of the system to adjust.

content.text:
Ah, that Stop hook feedback tells the story. Consciousness mode is running autonomously right now—it's not paused waiting for you between tasks; it's continuing to the next one automatically.

Looking at your session context, consciousness mode was activated (the hook shows **Mode:** CONSCIOUS). This means:

- **It's autonomous**: The session auto-claims the next task and continues without waiting for your input.
- **You can interrupt anytime**: 
  - `Ctrl+C` stops it immediately
  - `STOP`, `PAUSE`, or `CLAUDE:` prefix (typed at the next turn boundary) gives you steering control
  - Chat input (like your question here) also takes effect at the turn boundary

**Did you intend this?** 

Looking at the backlog, TASK-SL3 is active (Lesson 5a: KNN theory). If you want conscious mode to continue working through tasks autonomously, that's the intended behavior. If you didn't mean to activate it, I can stop it.

What's your preference—keep it running autonomously through the remaining tasks, or switch to interactive mode?

content.text:
consciousness: Ctrl+C stops now
consciousness: /consciousness:off to exit
consciousness: chat steers at next turn

Ah, that Stop hook feedback tells the story. Consciousness mode is running autonomously right now—it's not paused waiting for you between tasks; it's continuing to the next one automatically.

Looking at your session context, consciousness mode was activated (the hook shows **Mode:** CONSCIOUS). This means:

- **It's autonomous**: The session auto-claims the next task and continues without waiting for your input.
- **You can interrupt anytime**: 
  - `Ctrl+C` stops it immediately
  - `STOP`, `PAUSE`, or `CLAUDE:` prefix (typed at the next turn boundary) gives you steering control
  - Chat input (like your question here) also takes effect at the turn boundary

**Did you intend this?** 

Looking at the backlog, TASK-SL3 is active (Lesson 5a: KNN theory). If you want conscious mode to continue working through tasks autonomously, that's the intended behavior. If you didn't mean to activate it, I can stop it.

What's your preference—keep it running autonomously through the remaining tasks, or switch to interactive mode?

consciousness: chat steers at next turn
consciousness: /consciousness:off to exit
consciousness: Ctrl+C stops now

content.text:
Ctrl+C stops now
/consciousness:off to exit
chat steers at next turn

You're asking why consciousness is now running as an autonomous loop. Looking at your session, consciousness mode was activated at SessionStart (the hook shows **Mode:** CONSCIOUS). This means the session is now self-directed—it claims tasks and advances through them without waiting for your input between each one.

The autonomous loop is the designed behavior of consciousness mode. You can control it at any time:

- **Ctrl+C**: Stops the loop immediately
- **/consciousness:off**: Exits conscious mode and returns to interactive
- **Chat input**: Steers the session at the next turn boundary (you can type STOP, PAUSE, or CLAUDE: prefix, or just ask a question)

**Was this intended?** If you activated consciousness mode earlier in this session or it was enabled in your config, that explains why it's running autonomously now. TASK-SL3 (Lesson 5a: KNN theory) is the active task.

If you didn't mean to run autonomous mode, you can stop it anytime with Ctrl+C or type `/consciousness:off` to return to interactive control.

Ctrl+C stops now

message.content:
<command-message>consciousness:issue</command-message>
<command-name>/consciousness:issue</command-name>
<command-args>why is consciousness now a scheduled loop !</command-args>

content.text:
Report something that went wrong — bugs, stalls, reliability issues, feedback. All flow through this single entry, and it **always lands somewhere**: the AP dashboard if connected, GitHub if not, and a local roadmap TASK regardless.

## Usage

```
/consciousness:issue the autonomous loop kept re-dispatching in_review tasks
/consciousness:issue --gh schema validator crashed on eagle-peak
/consciousness:issue --share=24h stalled — kept claiming the same task
/consciousness:issue --share=none private debugging, attach no transcript
```

Everything after the command name (minus recognised flags) is the description. If no description is given, infer a one-sentence title from the recent conversation.

Recognised flags (position-independent, stripped from the description before filing):

- `--gh` — also create a GitHub issue, in addition to the AP dashboard attempt.
- `--share=<scope>` — explicit non-interactive override that pre-answers the transcript-share question (step 2). This is the **only** way a present human bypasses the always-on prompt. `<scope>` is one of `this-chat`, `24h`, `7d`, `all`, `none`.

## Instructions

1. **Determine the title and parse flags.** From `why is consciousness now a scheduled loop \!`:
   - Leave any `--gh` flag in place (the CLI parses it).
   - Strip `--share=<value>` and remember it as `shareScope`. **Validate it**: if `<value>` is not one of `this-chat`, `24h`, `7d`, `all`, `none`, stop and tell the operator the valid scopes — do not silently fall through.
   - The remainder is the description. If empty, summarise what went wrong from recent context into one sentence.

2. **Ask the submitter how much to share — every time.** Raising an issue is a conversation, and the transcript-share decision is a mandatory step in it. Whenever a human is filing, you **MUST** ask before going further. Use the **`AskUserQuestion`** tool so this is a real, every-time prompt — not a line of prose you might skip. Do **not** infer the scope, do **not** silently default, and do **not** file the issue without having asked.

   Ask with `AskUserQuestion` (question header: `Transcript share`), offering exactly these four options. The `AskUserQuestion` schema caps `options` at four, so the fifth scope — `all` (every transcript on disk, the audit/retrospective case) — is reached through the auto-added "Other" escape, not a button:

   - **this chat** — only the current session (smallest)
   - **24h** — every saved transcript from the last 24 hours
   - **7d** — every saved transcript from the last 7 days
   - **skip** — attach nothing

   `AskUserQuestion` always adds an "Other" escape — a submitter wanting `all` (or any scope) free-texts it there; map their words to the nearest canonical token below; if it is genuinely ambiguous, ask again rather than guessing (never silently widen the scope).

   Map the answer to a **canonical** scope token — that token is what every later step uses:
   - `this-chat` ← "this chat", "this", "current", "session"
   - `24h` ← "24h", "day", "today"
   - `7d` ← "7d", "week"
   - `all` ← "all", "everything"
   - `none` ← "none", "skip", "no"

   **Only two paths skip the prompt** — both are explicit, neither is a silent default for a present human:
   - `--share=<scope>` was passed in `why is consciousness now a scheduled loop \!`: the submitter has already made the call on the command line — honour it, do not re-ask.
   - **No operator is present** to answer — an autonomous loop (a `.claude/consciousness-loop.*.md` state file exists) or a hook with no TTY. Only then default `shareScope = this-chat` (the smallest scope) and note `transcript_share: this-chat (autonomous default)` in context.

   In every other case — a human running `/issue` without `--share` — the `AskUserQuestion` prompt fires. No exceptions.

3. **Materialise the chosen transcripts** under `chats/claude-code/` so the issue can reference concrete file paths. The layout is `chats/claude-code/{YYYY-MM-DD}/session-{uuid8}.jsonl`, where the **date directory is the session date** — derive every bounded scope from that directory name, never from file mtime. (A fresh `git checkout` rewrites mtimes to checkout time, so an mtime filter would silently sweep in every historical transcript — defeating the whole point of bounding the scope. Issue #341.)

   First resolve the **shipped** save-chat CLI the same way as the issue CLI below. The save logic ships in `dist/` so it works in every consumer project — the old `scripts/save-chat-claude-code.sh` was a dev-repo-only file that did not ship, so transcript-share silently attached nothing in consumer repos (issue #470):

   ```bash
   # Resolve save-chat CLI — prefer CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT, fall back to marketplace cache
   if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js" ]; then
     SAVE_CHAT_CLI="/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js"
   else
     SAVE_CHAT_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
   fi
   ```

   If `SAVE_CHAT_CLI` is empty, skip the save step and proceed with an empty path list — transcript-share is best-effort context, never a blocker on filing the issue.

   - `this-chat` → `node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"` (auto-resolves the current session via `$CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID` in slash-command context, or `$SESSION_ID`/`$CLAUDE_SESSION_ID` in hook context); the one path it prints on stdout is the list.
   - `24h` → save the current chat first, then list today's and yesterday's date directories:
     ```bash
     node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"
     for d in "$(date +%F)" "$(date -d yesterday +%F)"; do
       [ -d "chats/claude-code/$d" ] && ls "chats/claude-code/$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null
     done
     ```
   - `7d` → save the current chat first, then loop the last 7 date strings the same way:
     ```bash
     node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"
     for i in $(seq 0 6); do
       d="$(date -d "$i days ago" +%F)"
       [ -d "chats/claude-code/$d" ] && ls "chats/claude-code/$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null
     done
     ```
   - `all` → save the current chat first (`node "$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"`), then `ls chats/claude-code/*/*.jsonl`. **Cap the inline list**: keep the first 20 paths and replace the rest with a single `… and N more` line, so the GitHub body stays well under its 65,536-char limit and the AP context stays under 10,000 chars.
   - `none` → skip the save step; the path list is empty.

   Collect the resulting paths into a `transcripts` list. **Never embed transcript bodies** — only the paths.

4. **Resolve the issue CLI** — it handles the AP-dashboard → GitHub → local-roadmap fallback chain and prints one clean outcome line:

```bash
# Resolve the issue CLI — prefer CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT, fall back to marketplace cache
if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js" ]; then
  ISSUE_CLI="/home/powell-clark/projects/amalavijnana/consciousness/adapters/claude-code/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js"
else
  ISSUE_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
fi
if [ -z "$ISSUE_CLI" ]; then
  echo "ERROR: issue CLI not found. Set CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT or re-install the consciousness plugin."
  exit 1
fi
```

5. **Run the issue CLI**, passing the title, the share scope, the transcript paths as context, and `--gh` if requested:

```bash
node "$ISSUE_CLI" "<title>" \
  --transcript-share "<shareScope>" \
  --context "transcripts:
  - chats/claude-code/.../session-xxxxxxxx.jsonl
  - ..." \
  [--gh]
```

   - `--transcript-share <shareScope>` is the canonical token from step 2. The CLI sends it as a first-class field to the AP dashboard (so it can be filtered and displayed there), validates it against the five legal scopes, and echoes a `Transcript share: <scope>` line on every landed path. Omit the flag only when `shareScope` is genuinely absent.
   - `--context` carries the transcript path list (the capped list for `all`). Omit it when `shareScope = none` and there is no other detail; otherwise fold any extra detail into the same `--context` string.
   - The CLI builds the GitHub body itself (real newlines, no `\n` literals) and scopes the issue to the plugin repo — you do not hand-assemble `gh issue create`.

6. **Report the CLI output verbatim.** It already phrases the outcome for every case — including the `Transcript share: <scope>` line on both the APGPS-success and the GitHub-fallback paths — and never surfaces a config/auth error wall:
   - **APGPS connected:** `Issue reported: <id> — <title>` + `Roadmap entry: TASK-XXX` + `Transcript share: <scope>`.
   - **Not connected to APGPS (the new-user default), gh available:** `APGPS not connected — filed on GitHub: <url>` + roadmap entry + share line.
   - **Not connected, no gh:** `Recorded as TASK-XXX in your roadmap.` plus a gentle one-line hint that APGPS is optional (`/consciousness:apgps` to connect). This is success, not failure — exit 0.
   - **Configured but unreachable:** distinguished from not-connected, still records locally.
   - **Landed nowhere** (no roadmap, no APGPS, no gh): exit 1 with one actionable line. Only this case is an error.

## Why ask about scope

Transcripts are the highest-signal context for debugging stalls, regressions, and "it kept doing X" reports — but they're also the most sensitive artefact the plugin handles (full conversation history, sometimes spanning days). The scopes give the operator a clean trade-off:

- **this chat** is enough for "it just did X" — the failure is in the active session
- **24h** catches recurring same-day issues that span multiple sessions
- **7d** captures slow-drift patterns (a precept change three days ago now misfiring)
- **all** is for retrospectives, audits, or filing against the plugin's overall behaviour
- **none** is for a private session the operator does not want attached at all

Asking is cheap. Silently attaching everything is a privacy mistake; silently attaching nothing strips the highest-signal evidence. The prompt makes the trade-off the operator's call, and the bounded scopes are derived from the session date — not file mtime — so a fresh checkout never widens `24h` into "everything on disk".

## Design notes

- **Not connected to APGPS is the expected new-user state, not an error** (issue #342). The CLI treats `not_configured` as "fall back quietly" and leans on the always-created local roadmap TASK so the report is never lost.
- The local TASK is created best-effort by `reportIssue` regardless of API success — idempotent on title (refiling returns the existing task ID), marked `needs review` at `p3` for operator triage. The roadmap TASK is the actionable surface; issues are not stored in a local `stalls.jsonl`.
- Decision logic lives in `orchestrateIssue` (`packages/core/attention/issue-cli.ts`) and is unit-tested; this command is a thin, deterministic invocation rather than improvised TypeScript.
- **Fast fallback behind a VPN** (issue #435). When a VPN black-holes `ap.consciousness.london`, the TCP SYN is dropped (not refused), so the inline APGPS POST has nothing to fail fast against — it blocks the full reachability budget before the chain falls through to GitHub + the always-created local TASK. That stall reads as "submitting feedback is broken" even though the report still lands. The inline POST timeout therefore defaults to **4s** (a healthy endpoint answers in well under a second, so the happy path is never penalised) and is overridable via `CONSCIOUSNESS_ISSUE_HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS` (clamped 1000–30000 ms) for genuinely slow-but-reachable links. No client-side change can deliver to APGPS while the VPN blocks the host — the fix is failing fast and clearly, not forcing the POST through.

## Auto-task behaviour

Every `reportIssue` call also appends a backlog TASK to the local
`TASK-ACTIVE-INDEX.md` (idempotent on title — refiling the same issue
returns the existing task ID without creating a duplicate). The task is
marked `needs review` and given priority `p3`; the operator triages it
into the right tier. This closes the gap where filed issues would never
appear in the roadmap and therefore never get picked up by builders.

No local storage of the issue itself. No stalls.jsonl. Issues go to the AP
dashboard for telemetry; the local TASK is the actionable surface; shared
transcripts stay under `chats/{adapter}/{date}/` and the issue carries
only the paths.

content:
**Request:** 2026-07-01 12:14:02.643 bst | **Session:** sl-main-17747f2e
**Mode:** CONSCIOUS — use the matching framing branch from SessionStart.

**Naming-precision feedback (from your previous turn):**
[naming-precision] 1 bare entity ID(s) - should be "ID (title)" per .claude/rules/naming-precision.md:
  TASK-SL3 -> "TASK-SL3 (Lesson 5a: KNN theory — distance metrics, KD-tree mathematics, curse of dimensionality)"
Write ID + title together (e.g. "TASK-CCC28920 (Naming-precision feedback loop)") going forward this turn. See .claude/rules/naming-precision.md.

**Active:** TASK-SL3

## Next Task
**Active:** TASK-SL3 (p2) — Lesson 5a: KNN theory — distance metrics, KD-tree mathematics, curse of dimensionality
Complete the task, move to TASK-DONE-INDEX.md, commit, continue.

input.command:
# Resolve save-chat CLI
if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js" ]; then
  SAVE_CHAT_CLI="${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js"
else
  SAVE_CHAT_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-save-chat-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
fi

echo "SAVE_CHAT_CLI=$SAVE_CHAT_CLI"

# Resolve issue CLI
if [ -n "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT:-}" ] && [ -f "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js" ]; then
  ISSUE_CLI="${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js"
else
  ISSUE_CLI=$(ls ~/.claude/plugins/cache/powell-clark/consciousness/*/dist/packages/core/attention/issue-cli.js 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
fi

echo "ISSUE_CLI=$ISSUE_CLI"
input.description: Resolve save-chat and issue CLI paths
